faq:
Frequently Asked Questions

Can companion planting alone keep pests off my brassicas?

Not reliably. Companion planting reduced pest populations in only about 50 to 60 percent of studies, so for vulnerable crops like kale, broccoli, and cabbage, a row cover gives far more consistent protection.

Do I have to remove row covers when plants start flowering?

Yes, for crops that need pollination, such as squash, you must remove the cover once flowers appear so bees can reach the blossoms. Leaf crops like brassicas and lettuce can stay covered through harvest.

Is it worth using both methods together in a Santa Cruz garden?

Yes. Row covers handle immediate protection for at-risk crops, while companion plants like alyssum and yarrow build up beneficial insect populations over time, and our mild coastal climate keeps those beneficials active much of the year.

Frequently Asked Questions

Will adding calcium or crushed eggshells fix blossom end rot?

Usually not. The problem is rarely a lack of calcium in the soil but rather inconsistent watering that keeps the plant from moving calcium to the fruit, so calcium sprays and eggshells seldom help.

How can I tell if my tomatoes are getting enough water?

Check the soil about three inches deep, and if it feels dry, water more often. Coastal fog can make the surface look damp while the root zone stays dry, so always test below the surface.

Why does blossom end rot show up so often in early June here?

Early summer is when watering routines are still settling into a rhythm, and the inconsistency stresses fruiting plants. Containers, shallow raised beds, and sandy soils like those in the Pajaro Valley dry out quickly and are especially prone to it.

Frequently Asked Questions

Will coffee grounds acidify the soil around my blueberries?

No. Used grounds are nearly neutral at a pH of about 6.5 to 6.8 because brewing pulls most of the acid into the cup, so they will not meaningfully lower soil pH for acid-loving plants.

Can I use coffee grounds as a mulch?

It is best avoided. Applied in thick layers, grounds can compact into a water-repellent crust and may inhibit plant growth, which works against what you want from a mulch.

What actually lowers soil pH if I need more acidity?

Elemental sulfur is the reliable choice, applied based on a soil test and your target pH for the specific plant. Coffee grounds are better added to the compost pile, at no more than about 20 percent of the volume.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can gophers get through chicken wire?

Yes. Chicken wire has one to two inch openings that gophers can chew through or squeeze past, so it does not stop burrowing pests. Half-inch galvanized hardware cloth is the dependable choice underground.

Does chicken wire still have a place in the garden?

It does for above-ground jobs like lightweight seedling covers, protecting tree trunks from deer, and simple visual barriers. It is inexpensive and flexible, just not built for digging pests.

How should I line a raised bed to keep gophers out?

Line the bottom with half-inch, 19-gauge galvanized hardware cloth before adding soil, and for individual plants shape it into baskets at least 15 inches deep and wide. In our area gophers are a near certainty, so this upfront step is well worth it.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why is my rosemary turning yellow and dropping leaves?

These are classic signs of waterlogged roots, usually from overwatering. Rosemary is a Mediterranean plant that prefers to dry out between waterings, and too much moisture is the most common cause of decline in local gardens.

How often should I water rosemary?

Far less than most vegetables. For in-ground plants, keep rosemary off the same irrigation zone as your vegetables, and for containers, water only when the top two inches of soil are completely dry.

What kind of pot and soil work best for rosemary?

Use a pot with drainage holes and a fast-draining mix such as cactus soil. Terra cotta pots breathe and release moisture better than plastic, which helps protect the roots from rot.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which vegetables grow well in partial shade?

Leafy greens like lettuce, spinach, kale, chard, and arugula do well, along with herbs such as cilantro, parsley, and chives, and root crops like beets, radishes, and turnips. Most of these are happy with three to four hours of direct sun or bright filtered light.

Can I grow tomatoes in a shady spot?

Not really. Sun-loving crops like tomatoes, peppers, and squash need six or more hours of direct sun, so a shadier corner is better used for greens and herbs.

Is shade ever an advantage for a garden?

Yes. In hot inland areas partial shade can keep greens from bolting too early, and in the foggy coastal belt around Santa Cruz, choosing shade-tolerant varieties lets you make good use of naturally lower light.

Frequently Asked Questions

What can I plant in June in Santa Cruz County?

Succession-sow bush beans, summer squash, and cucumbers, and direct-sow sunflowers, zinnias, and cosmos. It is also time to start broccoli, cauliflower, and Brussels sprouts indoors for fall transplanting.

Why are my coastal tomatoes slow to ripen this month?

June fog keeps coastal temperatures cool, sometimes below 65 degrees, which slows ripening even as inland areas warm up. Cherry tomatoes in warmer inland spots usually start ripening by late June.

How much should I water tomatoes in June?

Aim for one to one and a half inches of water per week, delivered at the base of the plant, ideally through drip irrigation. Consistent watering helps prevent blossom end rot, cracking, and catfacing.

Frequently Asked Questions

Does cedar or redwood last longer for a raised bed?

Redwood generally lasts longer thanks to its denser heartwood, roughly 15 to 20 years in ground contact compared with about 10 to 15 years for cedar. Both are rated resistant to very resistant to decay.

Should I buy heartwood or is sapwood fine?

Always choose heartwood for either species. Sapwood breaks down quickly, in as little as three to five years, which undoes the durability advantage you are paying for.

Which wood makes the most sense for a Santa Cruz garden?

Redwood is a strong choice locally because it is regionally sourced, handles our wet-winter Mediterranean climate well, and supports local forestry. Cedar is a good option when budget is the main concern, and neither needs a sealant or liner when you use heartwood.
